---
title: "BIND configuration questions - root hints vs. forwarders"
date: 2014-11-17
forum: Server Platforms
---

### Post by Shub-Niggurath on 2014-11-17
Hello,
I'm a bit new to BIND and using a Chef cookbook to install it on an Ubuntu Server 14.04 node. The install is all fine and BIND is working fine, I just have a question about root hints vs. forwarders. I understand the difference between the two quite well, it's just the particular bit of BIND configuration that the Chef cookbook is giving me that is puzzling me.

Here's what my /etc/bind/named.conf looks like:
```
# Generated by Chef for
# Local modifications will be overwritten.
include "/etc/bind/named.rfc1912.zones";
include "/etc/bind/named.options";
include "/etc/bind/named.internalzones";


zone "." IN {
  type hint;
  file "named.ca";
};
```

And here's what /etc/bind/named.options looks like:
```
# Generated by Chef for
# Local modifications will be overwritten.


masters default {  };

options {
  directory "/var/cache/bind";
  dump-file "data/cache_dump.db";
  statistics-file "data/named_stats.txt";
  memstatistics-file "data/named_mem_stats.txt";

  recursion yes;
  allow-query { any; };
  forwarders { 10.200.184.2; };
  forward only;
  dnssec-enable yes;
  dnssec-validation yes;
  auth-nxdomain no;
};

logging {
  channel b_query {
    file "/var/log/named/query.log" versions 2 size 1m;
    print-time yes;
    severity info;
  };
  category queries { b_query; };
};
```

In a nutshell, what I'm wondering is what my DNS server is using to resolve records when I pass a query to it: the root hints defined in /etc/bind/named.conf, or the forwarder defined in /etc/bind/named.options? I know either works, but I **want** to use the forwarder in this instance, not root hints, and I don't know how to tell what it's doing to resolve my queries.

---

### Post by nerdtron on 2014-11-17
You can try querying a web page and let's see whom servers your query is being passed on to.
 
From the man page of dig: "man dig"
> 
    +[no]trace
           Toggle tracing of the delegation path from the root name servers for the name being
           looked up. Tracing is disabled by default. When tracing is enabled, dig makes iterative
           queries to resolve the name being looked up. It will follow referrals from the root
           servers, showing the answer from each server that was used to resolve the lookup.

           +dnssec is also set when +trace is set to better emulate the default queries from a
           nameserver.

So now let's try:
```
dig yahoo.com +trace
```

---

### Post by matt_symes on 2014-11-17
Hi

Do you need both forwarders and the root hints cache file ?

Kind regards

### Post by Shub-Niggurath on 2014-11-17
So first, let me state that between the time I posted this thread and now, I went ahead and removed the zone "." IN {}; statement from my /etc/bind/named.conf...

> **nerdtron said:**
> You can try querying a web page and let's see whom servers your query is being passed on to.
 
From the man page of dig: "man dig"


So now let's try:
```
dig yahoo.com +trace
```

The behavior I'm observing when I run that is aberrant, the trace is suggesting that I'm using root hints even though I totally removed the relevant settings from my named.conf file. It's resolving correctly but I'm no closer to figuring out how it's resolving, although I would think that now that I've removed the aforementioned statement from named.conf, I should only be using my forwarder... I don't see it happening in the trace, though. 10.200.188.39 in the output below is my internal BIND node.

Worse and weirder, when I run the same command on an internal host, the result returned by dig +trace is completely wrong, whereas it is correct when I run nslookup or dig without +trace.

```

The bolded bit at the bottom is wrong. I have a separate config for mydomain.com that forces requests to go to another set of forwarders which are also internal, so any queries for that domain should only go to those forwarders. The 66.96.162.198 returned by dig +trace is what you get if you query my  internal domain against public DNS (e.g. dig  myinternalhost.mydomain.com @8.8.8.8). I know the setup is working, since this is correct:

```
$ nslookup myinternalhost.mydomain.com.
Server:        10.200.188.39
Address:    10.200.188.39#53

Non-authoritative answer:
Name:    myinternalhost.mydomain.com
Address: 10.200.1.14
```

A simple ping also shows the hostname resolving to the correct internal IP.
So it's very weird to me that dig +trace returns different results from dig -trace. 
Aside from my internal DNS, though, I'm still confused as to the way my Ubuntu/BIND forwarder is resolving queries since +trace is showing that it's using the root servers even when I removed root hints from my main config file.

**Unless** it's behaving as designed and will always go to the root servers for anything when +trace is specified, ignoring the zone settings. That's what it looks like.

> **matt_symes said:**
> Hi

Do you need both forwarders and the root hints cache file ?

Kind regards

I do not, I only want to use forwarders in all situations. The thing is, though, the Chef recipe I'm using automatically adds the ```
zone "." IN {};
``` so I have to do a small hack in order to remove it forcibly, so my question aims to figure out if it's a bad idea to leave it alone in light of my desire only to use a forwarder that is defined in another spot of my config files. 

But like I said, I now no longer have the zone "." statement in my /etc/bind/named.conf, only the forwarders {}; statement in /etc/bind/named.options (as well as a bunch of zone-specific statements in my /etc/bind/named.internalzones to forward queries for specific internal domains to other internal DNS servers instead of the main one in /etc/bind/named.options).

---

### Post by matt_symes on 2014-11-17
Hi

Check out the man page for dig.

I can't copy and paste so I'll paraphrase and let you look it up for the exact wording.

> +trace : when trace is enabled it makes iterative queries to resolve the name being looked up
It will follow referrals from the root servers, showing the answer from each server that responds"

BTW: Only allow queries from IPs in your domain and you can have more than one forwarder IP address

Kind regards

---

### Post by Shub-Niggurath on 2014-11-18
> **matt_symes said:**
> Hi

Check out the man page for dig.

I can't copy and paste so I'll paraphrase and let you look it up for the exact wording.



I read that, thank you. I was hoping that it would actually show me all the steps involved in resolving my query, but it doesn't. Instead, it looks like adding +trace always forces a  query to go straight to the root servers, ignoring other settings  within the BIND configuration.
In this case I do have several internal domains set up in this fashion:
```
zone "myinternaldomain.com" {
        type forward;
        forwarders { 10.140.215.201; 10.20.216.200; 10.20.215.10; };
        forward only;
};
```

But  it appears that dig queries with [FONT=courier new]+trace[/FONT] ignore the forwarders listed  there, which is why [FONT=courier new]$ dig myinternaldomain.com[/FONT] or [FONT=courier new]$ nslookup myinternaldomain.com[/FONT] return the correct result, but [FONT=courier new]$ dig myinternaldomain.com +trace[/FONT] does not. Now, since I removed the root hints settings from my BIND config,  I guess it's safe to say that I'm using my desired forwarder at this  point, which is really all I care about.

> BTW: Only allow queries from IPs in your domain and you can have more than one forwarder IP address

Kind regards

Thanks for looking out. You are of course correct on both counts, but I'm handling this in a different fashion. I'm running this in AWS so I have a security group permitting DNS traffic only from certain hosts, and in any case the server is not accessible from the Internet, so whether or not I include an ACL statement makes no difference. I also only need one forwarder IP in this instance because Amazon VPC provides a highly-available DNS service accessible through a single IP address (which is your VPC CIDR ID + 2, e.g. if your VPC CIDR is 10.10.232.0/22, Amazon provides a DNS server for you at 10.10.232.2. A bit counter-intuitive but it works, I trust it, and it provides the lowest possible latency in this scenario, which is what I care about.

---

### Post by joebeasley on 2014-11-20
I would think all your queries would go to the forwarder.  You options are set to forward only.

 forwarders { 10.200.184.2; };
  forward only;

---
